vol_name: Pubertal development shapes perception of complex facial expressions
vol_description: We previously hypothesized that pubertal development shapes the emergence of new components of face processing (Scherf et al., 2012; Garcia & Scherf, 2015). Here, we evaluate this hypothesis by investigating emerging perceptual sensitivity to complex versus basic facial expressions across pubertal development. We tested pre-pubescent children (6–8 years), age- and sex-matched adolescents in early and later stages of pubertal development (11–14 years), and sexually mature adults (18–24 years). Using a perceptual staircase procedure, participants made visual discriminations of both socially complex expressions (sexual interest, contempt) that are arguably relevant to emerging peer-oriented relationships of adolescence, and basic (happy, anger) expressions that are important even in early infancy. Only sensitivity to detect complex expressions improved as a function of pubertal development. The ability to perceive these expressions is adult-like by late puberty when adolescents become sexually mature. This pattern of results provides the first evidence that pubertal development specifically influences emerging affective components of face perception in adolescence.

vol_name: Beyond the FFA: Brain-behavior correspondences in face recognition abilities
vol_description: Despite the thousands of papers investigating the neural basis of face perception in both humans and non-human primates, very little is known about how activation within this neural architecture relates to face processing behavior. Here, we investigated individual differences in brain-behavior correspondences within both core and extended regions of the face-processing system in healthy typically developing adults. To do so, we employed a set of behavioral and neural measures to capture a multifaceted perspective on assessing these brain-behavior relations. This included quantifying face and object recognition behavior, the magnitude and size of functional activation within each region, as well as a measure of global activation across regions. We report that face, but not object, recognition behavior was associated with 1) the magnitude of face-selective activation in the left FFA1, 2) larger face-related regions in multiple bilateral face-patches in the fusiform gyri as well as the bilateral anterior temporal lobe and amygdala, and 3) more distributed global face-network activation. In contrast, face recognition behavior was not associated with any measure of object- or place-selective activation. These findings suggest that superior behavior is served by engaging sufficiently large, distributed patches of neural real estate, which might reflect the integration of independent populations of neurons that enables the formation of richer representations.
vol_short_name: Individual Differences fMRI Visual Stimulation Task

vol_name: The Complex Emotion Expression Database: A Validated Stimulus Set of Trained Actors
vol_description:
The Complex Emotion Expression Database (CEED) is a digital stimulus set of 243 basic and 237 complex emotional facial expressions aimed at facilitating research in complex expression perception and processing. 

Stimulus Acquisition and Processing
The stimuli in CEED represent 6 basic expressions (angry, disgusted, fearful, happy, sad, and surprised) and 9 complex expressions (affectionate, attracted, betrayed, brokenhearted, contemptuous, desirous, flirtatious, jealous, and lovesick) that were posed by Black and White formally trained, young adult actors. The actors used a method acting approach and were provided with definitions and examples of each expression before they posed the expressions themselves. All of the images are greyscaled and standardized to a resolution of 300 dpi and a width of 4 inches. Some of the images were cropped to only include the head and neck of the actor, while others include the shoulders of the actor and some of the wall in the background.

Stimulus Validation
All images were validated by a minimum of 50 adults in a 4-alternative forced choice task (4AFC) that was administered on Amazon's Mechanical Turk. Only images for which greater than or equal to 50% raters endorsed the correct emotion label were included in the final database. Information regarding the number and gender of raters, the percentage of raters who chose each answer choice, and the answer choices given in the 4AFC task are provided in the folder "Stimulus Information."

Eye-Gaze FoCuS is a multi-part stimuli database that is optimized for investigating sensitivity to eye gaze. The paper describing the creation and validation of the stimuli is currently under review. The database includes two sets of stimuli and a table of information about video clips that can be extracted but are not included because of copyright laws.

Gaze Perception Stimuli
These stimuli were inspired by those developed by Riby and colleagues for investigating sensitivity to eye gaze cues in children and adolescents with autism and Williams syndrome (Riby et al., 2013). We designed these stimuli so that they could be used in a 4-alternative forced-choice task.  There are 245 images depicting actors gazing at objects in complex scenes. Each image depicts a single actor looking at one of many possible common objects in a scene. In addition to the target gazed-at object, there is always a plausible non-target object (i.e., nearby the target object but not gazed at), and several implausible objects (i.e., farther away from the target object and not gazed at) in each image. 

Gaze Following Stimuli
These stimuli were inspired by experimental paradigms that have typically been used in eye-tracking paradigms with young children and infants (see Bedford et al., 2012). There are 82 videos. In each video there is a gazed-at target object, a plausible non-target object that was spatially close to the target object but not the target of the gaze, and two implausible non-target objects that were not spatially close to the target object. The timing of events in each stimulus was carefully controlled. Each video begins with the actor looking straight into the camera for 2 seconds as if making eye contact with the participant in an episode of joint attention. Next, the actor shifts her gaze toward the target object (~500 msec). The actor then holds her gaze on the target object for 4 seconds. Finally, the actor shifts her gaze back toward the camera (~500 msec) and holds her gaze straight ahead at the camera for another 2 seconds. The entire gaze following video lasts approximately 9 seconds. Importantly, the actor never turns her head during the gaze shifts. 

Social Visual Attention Movie Clips
This database specifies timestamps for scenes from commercially-available movies that feature social interactions between adolescents and adults. 

All of these stimuli are available for research purposes. We also provide documentation containing the gazed-at and other objects and summary validation data for each image. For the social visual attention movie clips, we include descriptive and validation data.

vol_name: Introducing the Female Cambridge Face Memory Test – Long Form (F-CFMT+)
vol_description:
F-CFMT+ is designed to match all the parameters of the M-CFMT+ (Russell et al., 2009) at both the block (see Task Figure) and trial levels. The stimuli include high-resolution images of individual White women in the age range between 20-30 years. They were selected from the Radboud (Langner et al., 2010) and Karolinska (Lundqvist et al., 1998) faces databases. We selected images of multiple face identities from different viewpoints (frontal, 45, 135) to match the training trials in the M-CFMT+, and with different expressions to match the manipulations in the fourth block of the M-CFMT+. The images were converted to grayscale and cropped to exclude the background. The hair and all skin blemishes and discoloration (e.g., freckles) were removed from the faces. The 6 target white female faces were selected to exhibit a similar range of perceptual variability as exhibited among the 6 target white male faces in the M-CFMT+. As in the original CFMT and the M-CFMT+, the target identities appear on 1/6 trials throughout all four blocks of the task. Each target appears approximately 17 times throughout the task (range 16-18); the variability in rate of presentation is a function of the relative perceptual similarity between the target and the distractor identities. 

To create the task, each female target face (e.g., target face 1, target face 3) was substituted for the matching target male face (e.g., target face 1, target face 3) at the trial level throughout each block of the task. To match the M-CFMT+ task, 46 distractor identities were required with frontal and profile images. However, because of limitations with the stimuli databases that were available at the time of task development, only 38 distractor identities met these criteria. On average, each face appeared 5.37 times throughout the task. Therefore, the frequency of the distractor faces in the F-CFMT+ was a bit higher (1/19 trials) than in the M-CFMT+ (1/23 trials) in the first three blocks. 

	To make the noise-distorted images for Blocks 3 and 4, we applied a 30% level of Gaussian noise to the images, as described in the creation of the M-CFMT (Duchaine & Nakayama, 2006). However, given the high resolution of the female face images, we had to apply multiple iterations of this Gaussian noise to degrade the images to a similar level as in Blocks 3 and 4 of the M-CFMT+. In the final block 4, as in the M-CFMT+, we selected 14 of most frequently occurring distractor faces to repeat more frequently and increase the difficulty of this block by reducing “the difference in familiarity between target and distractor faces” (Russell et al., 2009). 

	Pilot testing on emerging adults at Penn State University was used to adjust the difficulty level of the task via selection of target and distractor items. Preliminary data with an earlier version of the task were published previously (Scherf et al., 2017). Results from this study were used to generate the power analysis and to refine the task for this round of testing and release.

This final version of the F-CFMT+ uses the same java code in which the M-CFMT+ was originally programmed. The instructions, task structure, and task timing are the exact same between tasks. Psychometric properties of the task are reported in Arrington et al (under review).

Update 11/04/25 - the Github folder linked in this volume also includes the data and code used in the social networks and face recognition paper (Arrington & Scherf, under review).

vol_name: Selective Tuning of the Visual System to the Waist-to-Hip Ratio: “First Pass” Fecundity and “Peak” Fertility Filters
vol_description:
These stimuli consist of 25 human female bodies and 25 sets of parallel lines. All stimuli were created for using SmithMicro Poser 10 and Adobe Photoshop software. Poser 10 is software for making 3D animated digital characters and is especially good at making high-resolution human anatomy. The female human body stimuli were created using the Alyson 2 professionally built character in Poser. We dressed the body in a leotard and positioned the arms away from the body to provide maximal viewing of the WHR. We blurred the face to avoid uncanny valley perceptions (MacDorman & Ishiguro, 2006; Mori, 1970).

The female body stimuli vary as a function of the waist-hip-ratio (WHR). It was important to vary the WHR without varying the perceived Body Mass Index (BMI), given previous work showing that preferences track separately with BMI and WHR (Tovee & Cornelissen, 2001; Tovee et al., 1998, 1999, 2002). To do so, we simultaneously and inversely altered the waist and hip regions of the figure (Streeter & McBurney, 2003; Koscinski, 2013; 2014). With each alteration, we incrementally increased the hip region (using the Hip Size and Hip Shape tools) and incrementally decreased the waist (using the Waist Width tool). This allowed us to create the set of figures with the full range of WHRs that varied incrementally by .01 from .66 - .90. For each body image, we created a non-social line version of the stimulus. To do so, we drew a pair of parallel lines across the waist and hips of the body in Adobe Photoshop so that the lines were the same distance apart, width of the body, and importantly, the same ratio to each other as the waist and hips of the body.  These pairs of parallel lines were overlaid on a white background, which created a set of line stimuli with the same incremental variation in ratio from .66 – .90
